name: Amanikable
description: Amanikable is a temperamental deity of the sea, originally worshipped by the Tagalog people of pre-colonial Philippines. He is often depicted as a fearsome god, known for his power over storms, waves, and the vast ocean. Unlike some other deities who are benevolent protectors, Amanikable is seen as a force of nature to be respected and feared. Some myths suggest he was once a mortal man who angered the gods and was transformed into a sea deity, forever bound to the ocean's whims. He is often associated with unsuccessful hunts and the dangers faced by fishermen at sea. In some narratives, he is considered the husband of Maganda, a goddess of beauty and the sea, although their relationship is often portrayed as turbulent. Amanikable's influence extends to the unpredictable nature of the sea, representing both its life-giving sustenance and its destructive potential. He is a reminder of humanity's vulnerability in the face of nature's raw power. Offerings and prayers were given to appease him, hoping to ensure safe voyages and bountiful catches. He embodies the darker, more untamed aspects of the marine realm, a constant reminder that the sea is not always a source of comfort and abundance.
subtitle: The Temperamental God of the Sea

keyMyths.title: The Transformation of Amanikable
keyMyths.description: Amanikable was once a mortal who angered the gods and was transformed into a sea deity. This transformation granted him control over the seas but also trapped him within its unpredictable nature.

keyMyths.title: Amanikable and Maganda
keyMyths.description: Amanikable's turbulent relationship with Maganda reflects the duality of the sea, both beautiful and dangerous. Their conflicts are said to cause storms and unpredictable tides.

keyMyths.title: Offerings to Amanikable
keyMyths.description: Fishermen would offer gifts to Amanikable to ensure safe passage and a bountiful catch. These offerings were a way to appease his temperamental nature.
keyMyths.source: Popular Stories and Legends of the Philippines

sources.author: Maximo Ramos
